question:
Let
\[
\begin{array}{cccc} a_{1,1} & a_{1,2} & a_{1,3} & \dots \\
a_{2,1} & a_{2,2} & a_{2,3} & \dots \\
a_{3,1} & a_{3,2} & a_{3,3} & \dots \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots
\end{array}
\]
be a doubly infinite array of positive integers, and suppose each
positive integer appears exactly eight times in the array. Prove that
$a_{m,n} > mn$ for some pair of positive integers $(m,n)$.
solution:
Solution. Suppose not; i.e., suppose that \( a_{m, n} \leq m n \) for all \( m, n \geq 1 \). Let
\[
R(k)=\left\{(i, j): a_{i, j} \leq k\right\} .
\]

By hypothesis, \( \# R(k) \leq 8 k \). On the other hand, \( R(k) \) contains all pairs \( (i, j) \) with \( i j \leq k \), and there are
\[
\left\lfloor\frac{k}{1}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor\frac{k}{2}\right\rfloor+\cdots+\left\lfloor\frac{k}{k}\right\rfloor>\left(\frac{k}{1}-1\right)+\left(\frac{k}{2}-1\right)+\cdots+\left(\frac{k}{k}-1\right)>k(\ln k-1)
\]
such pairs, since
\[
\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{k}>\int_{1}^{k} \frac{1}{x} d x=\ln k .
\]

Hence \( 8 k>k(\ln k-1) \), which is a contradiction for \( k>e^{9} \approx 8103.08 \).
Remark. To solve the problem, the explicit lower bound on the rate of growth of \( \frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{k} \) as \( k \rightarrow \infty \) is not really needed: it suffices to know that this sum tends to \( \infty \), i.e., that the harmonic series diverges.

descriptive_long.question:
Let
\[
\begin{array}{cccc} entryoneone & entryonetwo & entryonethree & \dots \\
entrytwoone & entrytwotwo & entrytwothree & \dots \\
entrythreeone & entrythreetwo & entrythreethree & \dots \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots
\end{array}
\]
be a doubly infinite array of positive integers, and suppose each
positive integer appears exactly eight times in the array. Prove that
$entrygeneric > rowmarker colmarker$ for some pair of positive integers $(rowmarker,colmarker)$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Suppose not; i.e., suppose that \( entrygeneric \leq rowmarker colmarker \) for all \( rowmarker, colmarker \geq 1 \). Let
\[
R(threshold)=\left\{(rowindex, colindex): entryvariable \leq threshold\right\} .
\]

By hypothesis, \( \# R(threshold) \leq 8\,threshold \). On the other hand, \( R(threshold) \) contains all pairs \( (rowindex, colindex) \) with \( rowindex\,colindex \leq threshold \), and there are
\[
\left\lfloor\frac{threshold}{1}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor\frac{threshold}{2}\right\rfloor+\cdots+\left\lfloor\frac{threshold}{threshold}\right\rfloor>\left(\frac{threshold}{1}-1\right)+\left(\frac{threshold}{2}-1\right)+\cdots+\left(\frac{threshold}{threshold}-1\right)>threshold(\ln threshold-1)
\]
such pairs, since
\[
\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{threshold}>\int_{1}^{threshold} \frac{1}{integvar} d integvar=\ln threshold .
\]

Hence \( 8\,threshold>threshold(\ln threshold-1) \), which is a contradiction for \( threshold>e^{9} \approx 8103.08 \).
Remark. To solve the problem, the explicit lower bound on the rate of growth of \( \frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{threshold} \) as \( threshold \rightarrow \infty \) is not really needed: it suffices to know that this sum tends to \( \infty \), i.e., that the harmonic series diverges.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let
\[
\begin{array}{cccc} silverfish & dragonfly & hummingbird & \dots \\
lemongrass & strawberry & alligator & \dots \\
porcupine & salamander & woodpecker & \dots \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots
\end{array}
\]
be a doubly infinite array of positive integers, and suppose each
positive integer appears exactly eight times in the array. Prove that
$dandelion > buttercup starflower$ for some pair of positive integers $(buttercup,starflower)$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Suppose not; i.e., suppose that \( dandelion \leq buttercup starflower \) for all \( buttercup, starflower \geq 1 \). Let
\[
R(cloudberry)=\left\{(raindrop, snowflake): watercress \leq cloudberry\right\} .
\]

By hypothesis, \( \# R(cloudberry) \leq 8\,cloudberry \). On the other hand, \( R(cloudberry) \) contains all pairs \( (raindrop, snowflake) \) with \( raindrop\,snowflake \leq cloudberry \), and there are
\[
\left\lfloor\frac{cloudberry}{1}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor\frac{cloudberry}{2}\right\rfloor+\cdots+\left\lfloor\frac{cloudberry}{cloudberry}\right\rfloor>\left(\frac{cloudberry}{1}-1\right)+\left(\frac{cloudberry}{2}-1\right)+\cdots+\left(\frac{cloudberry}{cloudberry}-1\right)>cloudberry(\ln cloudberry-1)
\]
such pairs, since
\[
\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{cloudberry}>\int_{1}^{cloudberry} \frac{1}{dragonfruit} d\,dragonfruit=\ln cloudberry .
\]

Hence \( 8\,cloudberry>cloudberry(\ln cloudberry-1) \), which is a contradiction for \( cloudberry>e^{9} \approx 8103.08 \).

Remark. To solve the problem, the explicit lower bound on the rate of growth of \( \frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{cloudberry} \) as \( cloudberry \rightarrow \infty \) is not really needed: it suffices to know that this sum tends to \( \infty \), i.e., that the harmonic series diverges.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let
\[
\begin{array}{cccc} negativedelta & oppositelambda & invertedgamma & \dots \\
contraryalpha & negativenu & reversekappa & \dots \\
opposingtheta & antiomicron & unlikezeta & \dots \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots
\end{array}
\]
be a doubly infinite array of positive integers, and suppose each
positive integer appears exactly eight times in the array. Prove that
distrustvalue > rowterminuscolumnfinal for some pair of positive integers (rowterminus,columnfinal).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Suppose not; i.e., suppose that \( distrustvalue \leq rowterminus columnfinal \) for all \( rowterminus, columnfinal \geq 1 \). Let
\[
R(boundupper)=\left\{(runoutindex, gonecounter): contraryentry \leq boundupper\right\} .
\]

By hypothesis, \( \# R(boundupper) \leq 8 boundupper \). On the other hand, \( R(boundupper) \) contains all pairs \( (runoutindex, gonecounter) \) with \( runoutindex gonecounter \leq boundupper \), and there are
\[
\left\lfloor\frac{boundupper}{1}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor\frac{boundupper}{2}\right\rfloor+\cdots+\left\lfloor\frac{boundupper}{boundupper}\right\rfloor>\left(\frac{boundupper}{1}-1\right)+\left(\frac{boundupper}{2}-1\right)+\cdots+\left(\frac{boundupper}{boundupper}-1\right)>boundupper(\ln boundupper-1)
\]
such pairs, since
\[
\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{boundupper}>\int_{1}^{boundupper} \frac{1}{abscissavalue} d abscissavalue=\ln boundupper .
\]

Hence \( 8 boundupper>boundupper(\ln boundupper-1) \), which is a contradiction for \( boundupper>e^{9} \approx 8103.08 \).

Remark. To solve the problem, the explicit lower bound on the rate of growth of \( \frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{boundupper} \) as \( boundupper \rightarrow \infty \) is not really needed: it suffices to know that this sum tends to \( \infty \), i.e., that the harmonic series diverges.

garbled_string.question:
Let
\[
\begin{array}{cccc} qzxwvtnp & hjgrksla & nbvcxmlu & \dots \\ fplmokij & sdrtyugh & weoplkjh & \dots \\ vbnmswer & ghtyrewq & zxcvbnml & \dots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots
\end{array}
\]
be a doubly infinite array of positive integers, and suppose each
positive integer appears exactly eight times in the array. Prove that
plmoknij > edcrfvtgyhbgtvfr for some pair of positive integers $(edcrfvtg,yhbgtvfr)$.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Suppose not; i.e., suppose that \( plmoknij \leq edcrfvtgyhbgtvfr \) for all \( edcrfvtg, yhbgtvfr \geq 1 \). Let
\[
R(tgbvfred)=\left\{(ujmnhygt, ikolpraw): qazwsxed \leq tgbvfred\right\} .
\]

By hypothesis, \( \# R(tgbvfred) \leq 8 tgbvfred \). On the other hand, \( R(tgbvfred) \) contains all pairs \( (ujmnhygt, ikolpraw) \) with \( ujmnhygt ikolpraw \leq tgbvfred \), and there are
\[
\left\lfloor\frac{tgbvfred}{1}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor\frac{tgbvfred}{2}\right\rfloor+\cdots+\left\lfloor\frac{tgbvfred}{tgbvfred}\right\rfloor>\left(\frac{tgbvfred}{1}-1\right)+\left(\frac{tgbvfred}{2}-1\right)+\cdots+\left(\frac{tgbvfred}{tgbvfred}-1\right)>tgbvfred(\ln tgbvfred-1)
\]
such pairs, since
\[
\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{tgbvfred}>\int_{1}^{tgbvfred} \frac{1}{olikujmy} d \,olikujmy=\ln tgbvfred .
\]

Hence \( 8 tgbvfred>tgbvfred(\ln tgbvfred-1) \), which is a contradiction for \( tgbvfred>e^{9} \approx 8103.08 \).
Remark. To solve the problem, the explicit lower bound on the rate of growth of \( \frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{tgbvfred} \) as \( tgbvfred \rightarrow \infty \) is not really needed: it suffices to know that this sum tends to \( \infty \), i.e., that the harmonic series diverges.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  

  A = (a_{(i},_j,_{k)})_{(i},_j,_k \geq  1_)  

be a triply-infinite array of positive integers indexed by ordered triples (i,j,k)\in \mathbb{N}^3.  
Assume the following two conditions.

1.  (Uniform multiplicity)  Every positive integer appears in A **exactly ten times**.  

2.  (Strong sub-polynomial bound)  For every (i,j,k) one has  
  a_{(i},_j,_{k)} \leq  \lfloor  (ijk)^{2/3} / log(ijk+1) \rfloor .  (\star )

Prove that these two requirements are incompatible; that is, show that there exists a triple (m,n,p) for which  

  a_{(m},_n,_{p)} > (mnp)^{2/3} / log(mnp+1).

(The logarithm is natural, but the base is irrelevant as long as it is fixed and greater than 1.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
We argue by contradiction.  Suppose an array A satisfies (1) and (2).

Step 1.  A bookkeeping set.  
For N\in \mathbb{N} define  

  R(N) := {(i,j,k)\in \mathbb{N}^3 : a_{(i},_j,_{k)} \leq  N}.      (1)  

Because each positive integer \leq  N occurs **exactly** ten times,  

  |R(N)| = 10 N.              (2)

Step 2.  A large subset of R(N).  
Put  

  X(N) := (N log N)^{3/2},    N \geq  4,   (3)  
  S(N) := {(i,j,k)\in \mathbb{N}^3 : i j k \leq  X(N)}.   (4)

We show that for every sufficiently large N one has S(N) \subseteq  R(N).  
Define  

  f(t) := t^{2/3}/log(t+1)  (t>0).    (5)

Claim.  For all N \geq  16 and all 0 < t \leq  X(N) we have f(t) \leq  N.

Proof of the claim.  
(a) The interval (0,7].  A direct calculation gives  

  max_{0<t\leq 7} f(t)=f(7)\approx 5.13<16\leq N.   (6)

(b) The interval [7, X(N)].  We verify that f is strictly increasing on [7,\infty ).  
Differentiating correctly,

  f'(t)=t^{-1/3}(log(t+1))^{-2} [(2/3) log(t+1)-t/(t+1)]. (7)

For t \geq  7 we have log(t+1) \geq  log 8 > 2, so  

  (2/3) log(t+1) \geq  (2/3)\cdot 2 = 4/3 > 1 \geq  t/(t+1),  

whence the bracket in (7) is positive and therefore f is increasing on [7,\infty ).

Consequently the maximum of f on [7,X(N)] is f(X(N)), and  

  f(X(N)) = X(N)^{2/3}/log(X(N)+1)  
      = N log N / log((N log N)^{3/2}+1).  (8)

Since log((N log N)^{3/2}+1) \geq  (3/2) log N for N \geq  4,  

  f(X(N)) \leq  N log N / ((3/2) log N) = (2/3)N < N. (9)

Combining (6) and (9) yields f(t) \leq  N for all 0<t\leq X(N), so by (\star ) we indeed have  

  S(N) \subseteq  R(N).             (10)

Step 3.  Counting triples with bounded product.  
Denote  

  T(X) := |{(i,j,k)\in \mathbb{N}^3 : i j k \leq  X}|.  

Write n = i j k.  Then T(X)=\sum _{n\leq X} d_3(n), where d_3(n) is the number of ordered factorizations of n into three positive integers.  
A three-dimensional Dirichlet hyperbola method (see e.g. Graham-Kolesnik, *Van der Corput's Method of Exponential Sums*, Thm. 1.9) gives  

  T(X)=\frac{1}{2} X (log X)^2 + O(X log X).    (11)

Hence there exist constants c>0 and X_0 such that  

  T(X) \geq  c X (log X)^2  for all X \geq  X_0. (12)

With X=X(N) from (3) and N large,

  |S(N)| = T(X(N)) \geq  c (N log N)^{3/2}(log N)^2  
          = c N^{3/2}(log N)^{7/2}.  (13)

Step 4.  Contradiction.  
Combine (2), (10) and (13):

  10 N = |R(N)| \geq  |S(N)| \geq  c N^{3/2}(log N)^{7/2}. (14)

Divide by N:

  10 \geq  c N^{1/2}(log N)^{7/2}.        (15)

The right-hand side tends to \infty  as N\to \infty , contradicting (15) once  

  N > max{16, X_0, (10/c)^2}.        (16)

Therefore no array can satisfy both (1) and (2).  Consequently there exists a triple (m,n,p) with  

  a_{(m},_n,_{p)} > (m n p)^{2/3} / log(m n p+1),

as required. \blacksquare 

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension: The problem escalates from a 2-dimensional array to a 3-dimensional one, enlarging the combinatorial enumeration from pairs to triples.  
• Sub-polynomial bound: The exponent 2⁄3 forces consideration of products ijk ≤ N^{3⁄2}, introducing non-linear rescaling absent in the original.  
• Asymptotic enumeration: Unlike the harmonic-series argument of the original, the proof now requires estimating the growth of T(X), the count of integer triples with bounded product.  This demands either the multi-dimensional Dirichlet hyperbola method or known bounds on summatory divisor functions – techniques beyond elementary series divergence.  
• Additional monotonicity constraint: Condition (3) intertwines the indices, necessitating an order-ideal argument to show that S(N) ⊆ R(N) and ensuring the lower bound used in (4).  
• Resulting complexity: Combining these elements yields growth of order X(log X)², leading to a contradiction only after careful analytic estimates, substantially deepening the theoretical framework compared with the original harmonic-series proof.

Step 4.  Contradiction.

  10 N = |R(N)| \geq  |S(N)| \geq  c N^{3/2}(log N)^{7/2}. (14)
